name: Filtering the Right Table in the WHERE Clause (LEFT JOIN conversion)
explain: Applying a WHERE filter to columns of the RIGHT table after a LEFT JOIN filters out the NULL values generated for unmatched rows. This effectively converts the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
WHERE d.status = 'Active'; -- Filters out NULL departments, killing LEFT JOIN behavior!

name: Omitting the JOIN Condition (Accidental Cartesian Product)
explain: Forgetting the ON clause or USING clause causes the database to perform a CROSS JOIN (Cartesian product), pairing every row of the first table with every row of the second table.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d; -- Missing ON condition

name: Using NATURAL JOIN on tables with shared metadata columns
explain: NATURAL JOIN matches tables on ALL columns with matching names. If both tables contain columns like 'created_at', 'updated_at', or 'status', it will attempt to match on those, leading to empty result sets.
bad:
SELECT * 
FROM orders
NATURAL JOIN customers; -- Matches on 'id' AND 'created_at', which is incorrect

name: Wrong Join Key (Joining on columns with different meanings)
explain: Accidentally joining on columns that have similar names but completely different business keys, such as joining an employee's department_id to a department's name.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.name; -- Mismatched types and keys

name: Missing Table Aliases with Duplicate Column Names
explain: When joining tables that have columns with identical names (e.g., 'id', 'name', 'created_at'), failing to use table aliases in the SELECT or ON clause results in an 'Ambiguous Column Name' error.
bad:
SELECT name, title
FROM employees
INNER JOIN projects ON project_id = id; -- Which table's 'id' or 'name'?

name: Accidental Row Multiplication (Joining on Non-Unique Keys)
explain: Joining tables on columns that contain duplicate values in both tables causes a many-to-many match, multiplying the output row count exponentially.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.location_id = d.location_id; -- Many employees and many departments share locations, leading to duplicated employee rows
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Join on unique primary key instead

name: Attempting to match NULL values in an ON clause
explain: In SQL, NULL represents an unknown value. The comparison 'NULL = NULL' evaluates to UNKNOWN (not TRUE). Therefore, rows with NULL keys will never match using standard equi-joins.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Fails to match employees where department_id IS NULL
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Keeps employees with NULL departments

name: Incorrect Join Order in Multi-Table Outer Joins
explain: If you follow a LEFT JOIN with an INNER JOIN, you can easily filter out the NULL records generated by the LEFT JOIN if the INNER JOIN filters on the same path.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name, p.title
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
INNER JOIN projects p ON d.project_id = p.id; -- If d.id is NULL, d.project_id is NULL. INNER JOIN on p.id fails, dropping the employee!
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name, p.title
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
LEFT JOIN projects p ON d.project_id = p.id; -- Keeps the optional chain intact

name: Using FULL JOIN in MySQL (Syntax Error)
explain: MySQL does not natively support FULL OUTER JOIN. Writing FULL JOIN directly in MySQL results in a syntax error.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Fails in MySQL
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
UNION
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Emulates FULL JOIN in MySQL

name: Placing Filters in the ON Clause of an INNER JOIN (Redundancy)
explain: In an INNER JOIN, placing filters in the ON clause vs the WHERE clause yields the exact same logical result, but putting non-joining logic in the ON clause hurts code readability.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id AND e.salary > 100000; -- Mixing join logic and filter logic
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
WHERE e.salary > 100000; -- Clean separation of concerns

name: Placing Filters on the LEFT Table in the ON Clause of a LEFT JOIN
explain: In a LEFT JOIN, putting a filter on the Left table inside the ON clause does NOT reduce the number of rows in the output. The Left table rows are still kept, but the Right columns will be padded with NULLs for rows that don't match the condition.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id AND e.country = 'USA'; -- USA employees get department info; other employees are still kept but have NULL departments!
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
WHERE e.country = 'USA'; -- Correctly filters output to show ONLY USA employees

name: Using DISTINCT to Patch a Bad Join Condition
explain: If your join is returning duplicate records, it is usually because the join keys are not unique. Relying on DISTINCT to fix the duplication is a performance anti-pattern that masks the underlying database relationship issue.
bad:
SELECT DISTINCT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.city = d.city; -- Using DISTINCT to hide duplicates generated by bad city match
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Proper primary key - foreign key match

name: Joining on Mismatched Data Types
explain: Joining a VARCHAR column to an INT column forces the database to perform implicit type conversion on every row, disabling index usage and causing severe performance degradation.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_code_string = d.id; -- Code string joined to integer id
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON CAST(e.dept_code_string AS INT) = d.id; -- Explicit conversion (if keys cannot be corrected in schema)

name: USING() Namespace Collisions
explain: While USING(id) is convenient, it requires that BOTH tables have a column with that exact name. If both tables have 'id' but in Table A it represents the primary key and in Table B it represents something else (like parent_id), it will match incorrectly.
bad:
SELECT * 
FROM tasks
INNER JOIN users USING(id); -- Matches tasks.id = users.id instead of tasks.user_id = users.id!

name: Circular Joins (Causing Infinite Loops / Redundancy)
explain: Joining table A to B, B to C, and then C back to A on the same keys is redundant and creates closed-loop relationships that can degrade performance and produce incorrect row counts.
bad:
SELECT *
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
INNER JOIN managers m ON d.manager_id = m.id
INNER JOIN employees e2 ON m.employee_id = e2.id AND e.id = e2.id; -- Redundant closing of loop
good:
SELECT *
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
INNER JOIN managers m ON d.manager_id = m.id;

name: Wrong Table on the Left side of a LEFT JOIN
explain: Using a LEFT JOIN but placing the table you want to filter/limit on the left, and the primary table on the right, resulting in missing primary records.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.department_id; -- Kept all departments; dropped employees without a department!
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Correct: Kept all employees

name: Implicit Join Syntax (Oracle old style comma join) in Modern Code
explain: Using commas in the FROM clause and placing join conditions in the WHERE clause is an outdated SQL-89 standard. It makes queries harder to read, error-prone, and can easily cause accidental Cartesian products.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e, departments d
WHERE e.department_id = d.id; -- Ancient comma join syntax
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Modern SQL-92 explicit syntax

name: Incorrect Aggregate calculations after a One-to-Many Join
explain: If you join Table A to a One-to-Many Table B and then SUM a column from Table A, the values from Table A will be duplicated for each matching row in Table B, yielding highly inflated values.
bad:
SELECT e.id, SUM(e.salary) AS total_salaries -- Salary is duplicated for every project employee has!
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.employee_id
GROUP BY e.id;
good:
SELECT e.id, e.salary, COUNT(p.id) AS project_count
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.employee_id
GROUP BY e.id, e.salary; -- Keep salary out of aggregate, or calculate totals prior to join

name: Joining on Inequality Operators without Bounds
explain: Joining using non-equi operators (like A.value > B.value) without upper/lower boundaries can lead to a massive fan-out of rows, causing out-of-memory errors on large tables.
bad:
SELECT *
FROM transactions t1
INNER JOIN transactions t2 ON t1.account_id = t2.account_id AND t1.amount > t2.amount; -- Matches every lower transaction, creating huge duplicates
good:
SELECT *
FROM transactions t1
INNER JOIN transactions t2 ON t1.account_id = t2.account_id AND t1.amount > t2.amount
WHERE t2.amount = (SELECT MAX(amount) FROM transactions WHERE account_id = t1.account_id AND amount < t1.amount); -- Limit matches to nearest neighbor

name: Using UNION instead of UNION ALL in simulated Full Outer Joins
explain: When emulating FULL OUTER JOIN using LEFT JOIN UNION RIGHT JOIN, using UNION (which removes duplicates) is much slower than UNION ALL because it performs an expensive sort operation, and it might remove valid duplicates.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
UNION
SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Triggers slow duplicate sorting
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
UNION ALL
SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id WHERE e.department_id IS NULL; -- Faster, mathematically identical, and does not sort

name: Assuming Right Join is a distinct operation from Left Join
explain: Believing that RIGHT JOIN offers unique capabilities that LEFT JOIN does not, resulting in unreadable queries. A RIGHT JOIN is logically identical to swapping table positions in a LEFT JOIN.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Less readable (we read left-to-right)
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.department_id; -- Clean and readable equivalent

name: Joining on Subqueries instead of joining directly
explain: Creating subqueries for simple table reads and joining on them is an anti-pattern. Most modern database optimizers perform better when tables are joined directly so indexes can be leveraged directly.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN (SELECT * FROM departments) d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Redundant subquery
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Clean direct join

name: Accidentally turning CROSS JOIN into INNER JOIN
explain: Specifying a CROSS JOIN but then adding a join filter in the WHERE clause defeats the purpose of the CROSS JOIN statement and should be written as a standard INNER JOIN for clarity.
bad:
SELECT e.name, p.title
FROM employees e
CROSS JOIN projects p
WHERE e.project_id = p.id; -- Confuses reader, looks like Cartesian product but runs as Inner Join
good:
SELECT e.name, p.title
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN projects p ON e.project_id = p.id; -- Explicit and readable

name: Incorrect SELF JOIN comparison operator (Infinite match)
explain: Self-joining a table to find sibling rows or matching records but failing to exclude matching the row to itself.
bad:
SELECT e1.name, e2.name
FROM employees e1
INNER JOIN employees e2 ON e1.department_id = e2.department_id; -- Matches John with John, Sarah with Sarah, etc.
good:
SELECT e1.name, e2.name
FROM employees e1
INNER JOIN employees e2 ON e1.department_id = e2.department_id
WHERE e1.id <> e2.id; -- Excludes matching the same employee

name: Hardcoding JOIN keys instead of using columns
explain: Using literal string matches or static IDs in the ON clause instead of mapping the primary key and foreign key variables.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = 5; -- Hardcoded ID in join clause
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
WHERE d.id = 5; -- Correct join key mapping with WHERE filter

name: Applying OR in ON clauses blindly
explain: Using an OR condition in a JOIN's ON clause (e.g., ON a.id = b.id OR a.email = b.email) prevents the database query planner from utilizing indexes effectively, often causing full table scans.
bad:
SELECT u.name, c.company_name
FROM users u
INNER JOIN companies c ON u.company_id = c.id OR u.email = c.domain_email; -- Extremely slow join
good:
SELECT u.name, c.company_name FROM users u INNER JOIN companies c ON u.company_id = c.id
UNION
SELECT u.name, c.company_name FROM users u INNER JOIN companies c ON u.email = c.domain_email; -- Optimizable index paths

name: Assuming NULL keys match in LEFT JOIN
explain: Believing that a LEFT JOIN will automatically match rows where the join keys are NULL on both sides. NULLs are non-equal by definition.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Does NOT match employees with NULL department_id to departments with NULL IDs
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id OR (e.department_id IS NULL AND d.id IS NULL); -- Explicitly handles matching NULLs

name: Omitting join alias prefix in SELECT when selecting columns
explain: Selecting columns without specifying the table alias when columns are unique *now* but could be duplicated in the future as database schemas evolve.
bad:
SELECT name, location
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Works now, but if 'location' is added to employees table later, query breaks!
good:
SELECT e.name, d.location
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Future-proof and safe

name: Joining on strings without trimming whitespace
explain: Joining tables on text fields (like email or username) without stripping whitespace or matching case sensitiveness, leading to missed matches.
bad:
SELECT u.name, p.profile_pic
FROM users u
INNER JOIN profiles p ON u.email = p.email; -- Fails if user entered ' john@eg.com ' with spaces
good:
SELECT u.name, p.profile_pic
FROM users u
INNER JOIN profiles p ON TRIM(LOWER(u.email)) = TRIM(LOWER(p.email)); -- Standardized join match

name: Writing Nested Joins without parenthesis
explain: Writing multi-table joins where the logical evaluation order is ambiguous, leading to unexpected results based on optimizer default parsing rules.
bad:
SELECT e.name, d.name, p.title
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d
INNER JOIN projects p ON d.project_id = p.id ON e.department_id = d.id; -- Confusing syntax order
good:
SELECT e.name, d.name, p.title
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
INNER JOIN projects p ON d.project_id = p.id; -- Sequential, readable structure
